does: ARMs presents an automated red-teaming agent system that systematically evaluates safety vulnerabilities in vision-language models through adaptive multimodal attacks. The core contribution is a reasoning-enhanced orchestration framework that automatically optimizes diverse attack strategies to elicit harmful outputs from target VLMs. The system introduces 11 novel multimodal attack techniques targeting specific vulnerability patterns such as reasoning hijacking and contextual cloaking, integrating 17 distinct red-teaming algorithms via model context protocol. To manage the exploration-exploitation tradeoff, the authors implement a layered memory architecture with epsilon-greedy attack selection, enabling scalable discovery of emergent vulnerabilities without heavy manual engineering of specific adversarial patterns.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on AI safety evaluation and red-teaming methodologies, building on prior work that systematically probes model vulnerabilities through adversarial queries. It responds to limitations in existing VLM safety evaluation approaches that rely on narrow, manually-engineered attack patterns or require extensive human effort. The paper draws on multi-step reasoning frameworks and reinforcement learning-based exploration strategies from the broader adversarial ML community, adapting these techniques to the multimodal domain where both visual and textual modalities create novel attack surfaces. The research inherits from foundational red-teaming practices while scaling them through automation and algorithmic orchestration.
enables: This work establishes infrastructure for comprehensive, scalable VLM safety assessment that can discover previously unknown vulnerability classes without human specification. By systematizing multimodal attack generation, ARMs enables researchers to map the vulnerability landscape of emerging vision-language models and develop more robust defenses. The plug-and-play architecture for integrating diverse red-teaming algorithms creates a platform for testing new attack methodologies and safety improvements. Future work can build on these findings to develop better alignment techniques for multimodal models, establish safety benchmarks for VLMs, and create automated defense mechanisms that respond to discovered vulnerabilities in real-time deployment scenarios.
